Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon, but it was pretty scattered. Some weeks I'd put in a lot of hours just scrolling through materials, other weeks barely anything. I know I logged about 23 hours a week supposedly, but a lot of that was just having stuff open while I was doing other things or zoning out.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly panic when I realized something was due and cram everything in. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Regular study times? That's not really how I operate, even though I know it would probably help.
Q3: Usually it was fear, to be honest. I'd get that anxious feeling in my stomach like I was forgetting something important, and I'd log in to check what was due. Sometimes I'd just log in out of habit or boredom, scroll around without really doing anything productive. The notifications helped remind me too, but I didn't always act on them.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became kind of automatic after a while. I'd just open it up like checking social media, not really with a purpose. It happened because I was scared of missing something important, so I kept checking even when I wasn't actually going to do any work. It was more like a nervous habit than actual engagement.
Q5: The reading materials were most useful for me since I learn better that way. I could read through stuff at my own pace and highlight things. The discussion forums were okay too because I could see what other people were confused about, which made me feel less alone in not understanding things. Videos were hit or miss depending on how long they were.
Q6: I barely touched the longer videos, honestly. If something was over 20 minutes, I'd just skim or skip it entirely. Some of the optional readings I ignored completely because I was already overwhelmed with the required stuff. I didn't have the energy or motivation to go beyond what was absolutely necessary most of the time.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to an assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. Anything that seemed like busy work or just extra fluff, I'd skip. I also needed things to be relatively quick and clear. If something took forever to get to the point, I'd lose interest fast and move on to something else.
Q8: Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. Like, I was already struggling to keep up with required work, so optional activities were the first thing to go. Required things had that fear factor attached to them, the threat of failing. Optional stuff didn't have that same urgency, so my brain just deprioritized it automatically.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my laptop and study from there without being online. I have limited data sometimes, so working offline made sense. I also used my own notes a lot, just writing things out by hand helped me remember better than staring at a screen. None of that would show up anywhere.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS wouldn't know about any of it. I'd also think through problems while walking or doing other stuff, working things out in my head. That kind of mental engagement doesn't leave any digital trace.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a PDF once to download it, then read it multiple times offline over the next few days. The LMS would just show one quick access, but I'd actually spend hours with that material. It was more practical for me given my resources and the way I prefer to study.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. I felt scared and overwhelmed, like I was drowning and couldn't catch up. My motivation would tank completely during those times. I'd log in, see all the stuff I was behind on, and just close it again because it was too much to face.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or had other stuff going on, the LMS became this thing I'd avoid because it just reminded me of everything I wasn't doing. Confusion was a big one too. If I didn't understand something, I'd put off engaging with it rather than asking for help. My limited resources meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to either.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing in the background while I was actually on my phone or thinking about other stuff. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually absorbing anything, just going through the motions. It looked like engagement but my brain was somewhere else entirely.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety would build up and make it harder to start, not easier. It's like this paralysis where I knew I should do it but I just couldn't make myself begin. The fear of doing it wrong was sometimes worse than the fear of not doing it at all.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me actually do work. I'd ignore stuff for weeks, then frantically try to complete everything in the last day or two. The LMS would probably show these huge spikes of activity right before due dates. It's not healthy, but it's the only way I seemed to function.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it could go either way. Good feedback made me feel okay for a bit, like maybe I wasn't totally failing. Bad feedback or criticism made me want to avoid the course even more. I didn't always check feedback right away because I was scared of what it might say.
Q18: The structure was okay, but I didn't always follow it the way it was intended. If things were spread out, I'd procrastinate. If too much was due at once, I'd get overwhelmed and shut down. I needed more flexibility but also more external pressure, which is kind of contradictory, I know.
Q19: Having clear, short modules made things easier. When expectations were vague or materials were scattered everywhere, it was harder to engage. The discussion requirements actually helped because they forced me to at least show up and say something. But the sheer number of online courses I was taking made everything blur together.
Q20: That sounds like me, honestly. I'd be on the LMS a lot, clicking around, watching bits of things, participating in discussions, but then not actually submitting the final assignments. It's like I could engage with the low-stakes stuff but the high-stakes submissions felt too scary or overwhelming to complete.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone's studying mostly offline or just really efficient, they might not need to be on the LMS much. For me, I was on there a lot but it didn't always translate to good work. Being online doesn't equal learning, and not being online doesn't mean you're not learning.
Q22: Logs can show when you're clicking around and how often, but they can't show what's actually happening in your head. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the anxiety that keeps you from engaging even when you're logged in. They see the surface but not what's underneath.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through things. For me, true engagement is rare. Most of the time I'm just trying to survive and get through, not genuinely connecting with the material.
Q24: The fear. The LMS can't show how scared I am most of the time, how that affects everything I do or don't do. It can't show the offline studying, the mental energy spent worrying instead of working, or how much the CBT sessions have actually helped my mood even when my grades don't reflect it. There's a whole internal struggle that's invisible.
